topic: Philosophy of Religion: Problems of Evil & Suffering
subject: religious-studies

items.q: Is the problem of evil only about logical contradiction?
items.correct: No - the evidential version, pressed by Rowe with cases like a fawn dying slowly in a forest fire, argues that apparently pointless suffering makes God improbable even if no strict contradiction exists.
items.misconception: Once the logical problem is answered, the problem of evil is completely solved.
items.random_wrong: The problem of evil was first invented in the twentieth century.
items.why: Plantinga's reply to Mackie is often treated as the end of the debate; the evidential problem needs separate treatment.

items.q: Does soul-making neatly explain every amount of suffering?
items.correct: No - critics object that some evil crushes rather than develops people and seems excessive; Hick himself appeals to mystery and universal salvation to respond.
items.misconception: Hick's theodicy shows that every instance of suffering produces growth.
items.random_wrong: Hick argued that suffering is a punishment for past lives.
items.why: Students present theodicies as airtight; the marks lie in testing them against dysteleological, excessive evil.

items.q: Did Augustine teach that God created evil?
items.correct: No - for Augustine creation was 'very good' and evil is a privation arising from free creatures turning away from God, not a substance God made.
items.misconception: Augustine believed God deliberately built evil into creation.
items.random_wrong: Augustine denied that anyone ever suffers.
items.why: The privation doctrine exists precisely to keep God from being evil's author; missing it inverts Augustine's whole position.

items.q: What does Mackie's logical problem of evil claim?
items.correct: That God's omnipotence, God's goodness and the existence of evil cannot all be true together
items.random_wrong: That suffering proves there are many gods
items.why: Correct answer: That God's omnipotence, God's goodness and the existence of evil cannot all be true together. “That evil is an illusion” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Which of these is an example of moral evil?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Genocide. “A tsunami” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: In Hick's theodicy, what is 'epistemic distance'?
items.correct: God remains hidden enough for humans to choose faith and goodness freely
items.misconception: The physical distance between heaven and earth
items.random_wrong: The gap between rich and poor
items.why: The answer examiners want is: God remains hidden enough for humans to choose faith and goodness freely. “The physical distance between heaven and earth” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: What does the free will defence argue?
items.correct: Genuine freedom is so valuable that God is justified in permitting the evil its misuse brings
items.random_wrong: God causes every evil act directly
items.why: Correct answer: Genuine freedom is so valuable that God is justified in permitting the evil its misuse brings. “Humans have no free will” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
